title: Manage the Do Not Call List
lede: One list, zero accidental dials. Every outbound call iMash places — campaign dials, workflow calls, API calls — is checked against your Do Not Call list first, so a number you block here can never be dialed again. Here's how to build and maintain it in minutes.

steps.title: Open the Do Not Call list
steps.content:
Your Do Not Call (DNC) list is your account-wide safety net for calling compliance. Let's find it first.

  1. In the top navigation bar, click Contact Center.
  2. In the tab strip that appears below it, click Do Not Call (the red tab — it also shows a live count of blocked numbers).

The Do Not Call List page opens. Each row is one blocked phone number, with these columns:

  • Phone Number — the blocked number. Click the column header to sort.
  • Added Date — when the number was added to the list.
  • Contact ID / Lead ID — if the number matches a contact or lead in your CRM, their ID appears as a clickable link (more on this in a later step). A dash means no match.
  • Call Log — a green eye icon appears if there's a past call to this number; click it to review that call.
  • Owner — which account or team member the entry belongs to. Click the pencil icon to reassign it.
  • Actions — the red trash icon removes the number from the list.

images.alt: The Do Not Call List page showing blocked numbers with their added dates, CRM matches, owners, and actions
images.caption: The Do Not Call list — every number here is automatically blocked from all outbound dialing

images.alt: The Do Not Call List page showing blocked numbers with their added dates, CRM matches, owners, and actions
images.caption: The Do Not Call list — every number here is automatically blocked from all outbound dialing

steps.title: Get to know the toolbar
steps.content:
Everything you'll use day-to-day sits in one row above the table:

  • Search phone numbers — type any part of a number to filter the list instantly. Great for a quick "is this number blocked?" check before a call.
  • Filter by owner — narrow the list to entries belonging to a specific team member or sub-account.
  • Import Numbers — add numbers in bulk from a file or by typing them in (next two steps).
  • API Info — opens a ready-made API cheat sheet for adding, listing, updating, and deleting DNC numbers programmatically.
  • Delete Selected — this button appears only after you tick one or more row checkboxes, and removes them all at once.

images.alt: The Do Not Call toolbar with the phone number search box, owner filter, Import Numbers button, and API Info button
images.caption: Search, owner filter, Import Numbers, and API Info — your DNC toolkit

images.alt: The Do Not Call toolbar with the phone number search box, owner filter, Import Numbers button, and API Info button
images.caption: Search, owner filter, Import Numbers, and API Info — your DNC toolkit

steps.title: Import numbers from a file
steps.content:
Have an existing suppression list? Load it in one shot:

  1. Click Import Numbers in the top-right of the page.
  2. In the Import Do Not Call Numbers window, stay on the Upload File tab.
  3. Click or drag a .txt or .csv file into the upload area. The file just needs phone numbers — one per line, or separated by commas or semicolons.
  4. Click Import Numbers. A success message confirms how many numbers were added.
💡 Tip: Duplicates are skipped automatically, so it's completely safe to re-import the same list after adding new numbers to it — nothing gets double-counted.

images.alt: The Import Do Not Call Numbers window on the Upload File tab with a drag-and-drop area for .txt or .csv files
images.caption: Drag in a .txt or .csv file, then click Import Numbers

images.alt: The Import Do Not Call Numbers window on the Upload File tab with a drag-and-drop area for .txt or .csv files
images.caption: Drag in a .txt or .csv file, then click Import Numbers

steps.title: Add numbers by typing or pasting them
steps.content:
For one-off additions — say, a customer just asked to stop being called — manual entry is fastest:

  1. Click Import Numbers.
  2. Switch to the Manual Input tab.
  3. Type or paste the numbers into the text box — one per line, or separated by commas or semicolons. Any common format works (digits, +, dashes, parentheses, spaces).
  4. Click Add Numbers.

The new numbers appear at the top of the list immediately, and are blocked from that moment on.

images.alt: The Import Do Not Call Numbers window on the Manual Input tab with a large text box for typing or pasting phone numbers
images.caption: Paste numbers on the Manual Input tab and click Add Numbers

images.alt: The Import Do Not Call Numbers window on the Manual Input tab with a large text box for typing or pasting phone numbers
images.caption: Paste numbers on the Manual Input tab and click Add Numbers

steps.title: See who's behind a blocked number
steps.content:
The list doesn't just store numbers — it connects them back to your CRM so you always know who asked not to be called:

  • Click a blue ID link in the Contact ID or Lead ID column to open that contact's or lead's full record in a side panel — their details, activity, and history — without leaving the page. If a number matches several records, you'll see one link per match.
  • Click the green eye icon in the Call Log column to open the most recent call to that number, including the recording and transcript — handy for verifying exactly when and how the person asked to opt out.

steps.title: Remove numbers from the list
steps.content:
If someone opts back in, or a number was blocked by mistake:

  1. Single number: click the red trash icon in the Actions column, then confirm with Yes, Delete.
  2. Several at once: tick the checkbox on each row (use Search phone numbers first to find them), then click Delete Selected and confirm.
⚠️ Warning: Removing a number makes it immediately dialable again by every campaign and workflow. Only remove a number when you're certain the person has consented to be contacted again.

images.alt: The Delete Number confirmation dialog asking to confirm removing a phone number from the Do Not Call list
images.caption: Deletions always ask for confirmation before the number becomes dialable again

images.alt: The Delete Number confirmation dialog asking to confirm removing a phone number from the Do Not Call list
images.caption: Deletions always ask for confirmation before the number becomes dialable again

steps.title: How blocking actually works (the best part: it's automatic)
steps.content:
Here's the magic — you never have to scrub a dial list by hand again. Every single outbound call placed through iMash passes a DNC check before it dials:

  • Campaign calls — the AI dialer skips any list entry whose number is blocked.
  • Workflow & automation calls — calls triggered by your Smart Flows are checked too.
  • API calls — calls started programmatically go through the same gate.

When a blocked number is hit, the call is stopped before it ever rings, and a call log entry with the ended reason Do Not Call is recorded — so you have an audit trail proving the number was suppressed, not skipped by accident.

Matching is format-smart: +1 (555) 123-4567, 15551234567, and 5551234567 are all treated as the same number, so you don't need to normalize your imports.

💡 Compliance tip: Import your existing suppression list before launching your first campaign, and add opt-out requests the moment they come in. Honoring do-not-call requests promptly is a legal requirement in most regions (TCPA in the US), and this list is your single source of truth for it.

steps.title: Let your AI agent and the API keep the list growing
steps.content:
The list also maintains itself:

  • AI-detected opt-outs: when someone tells your AI voice agent to stop calling, the call is filed under the Do Not Call stage and the number is added to this list automatically — no human action required. The matching lead or contact keeps its red Do Not Call stage in the CRM too.
  • API: click API Info in the top-right to open an interactive reference for the DNC endpoint. It shows your endpoint URL, supported operations (ADD, LIST, UPDATE, DELETE), a request builder, and copy-paste code examples in cURL, JavaScript, Python, PHP, Ruby, and Go. Use it to sync suppression lists from another system on a schedule.

With imports, AI auto-adds, and the API all feeding one list, compliance stops being a chore — it just runs in the background while you dial with confidence.

images.alt: The API Info window for the Do Not Call List showing the endpoint URL, supported operations, request builder, and code examples
images.caption: The API Info window — everything you need to manage the DNC list programmatically

images.alt: The API Info window for the Do Not Call List showing the endpoint URL, supported operations, request builder, and code examples
images.caption: The API Info window — everything you need to manage the DNC list programmatically

metaTitle: Manage the Do Not Call List | iMash Documentation
metaDescription: Build and maintain your iMash Do Not Call list: import numbers from a file, add them manually, let your AI agent capture opt-outs automatically, and block every outbound campaign, workflow, and API call to suppressed numbers.
